does: This paper reformulates automatic speech recognition as an interactive multi-turn refinement task rather than a single-pass transcription problem. The authors propose Agentic ASR, a closed-loop framework that pairs conventional ASR output with semantic correction mechanisms, intent routing, and reasoning-based editing to iteratively improve transcriptions. They introduce Sentence-level Semantic Error Rate (S²ER), an LLM-based evaluation metric that assesses semantic correctness rather than token-level accuracy, and develop an Interactive Simulation System for benchmarking agentic ASR systems at scale. Experiments demonstrate improvements across multilingual datasets, named-entity-intensive domains, and code-switching scenarios.
connects: This work extends traditional ASR research, which has long relied on edit-distance metrics like word error rate and character error rate, toward more semantically grounded evaluation. It builds on recent advances in LLM-based agents and reasoning systems that enable multi-step problem-solving, and connects to work on clarification dialogues and error recovery in dialogue systems. The paper responds to the growing integration of ASR with large language models in conversational AI, where semantic correctness matters more than transcription fidelity. It also engages with research on human-computer interaction that emphasizes iterative understanding and repair mechanisms central to natural communication.
enables: This framework opens pathways for more robust conversational AI systems that can recover from misrecognition through agentic reasoning rather than requiring users to repeat themselves. It enables development of ASR systems tailored to high-stakes domains like medical dictation or code transcription where semantic errors have significant consequences. The S²ER metric could become a standard for evaluating ASR in semantic-critical applications, shifting the field away from token-level metrics. The interactive simulation system supports scalable testing of refinement strategies and error recovery approaches. Future work could extend this to fully integrated dialogue systems where speech recognition, understanding, and generation form a unified agentic loop with continuous user feedback.
